The figure is generated using the 'diamonds' dataset from the 'ggplot2' package. It's a grouped bar chart with 'cut' on the x-axis and the count of diamonds on the y-axis. The color of the bars represents the 'clarity' of the diamonds. 

In order to replicate the figure, one should consider the following details: 

1. The dataset - 'diamonds' dataset from the 'ggplot2' package
2. Libraries/packages - 'plotly', 'ggplot2', and 'dplyr'
3. X-axis - 'cut' of the diamonds
4. Y-axis - Count of diamonds in each category of 'cut'
5. Color scheme - based on the 'clarity' of diamonds
6. Type of chart - Grouped bar chart
7. Data manipulation - The dataset is grouped by 'cut' and 'clarity', and the number of diamonds in each combination is calculated.

Remember, no additional variables, functions, or random seed is used in this code.
code_reference:
library(plotly)
library(dplyr)

fig <- ggplot2::diamonds
fig <- fig %>% count(cut, clarity)
fig <- fig %>% plot_ly(x = ~cut, y = ~n, color = ~clarity)

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Load the diamonds dataset
url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mwaskom/seaborn-data/master/diamonds.csv"
diamonds = pd.read_csv(url)

# Group data by 'cut' and 'clarity' and count occurrences
grouped_data = diamonds.groupby(['cut', 'clarity']).size().unstack(fill_value=0)

# Define colors for clarity levels
clarity_order = ['I1', 'SI2', 'SI1', 'VS2', 'VS1', 'VVS2', 'VVS1', 'IF']
colors = sns.color_palette("viridis", len(clarity_order))

# Create the figure and axis
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
ax = plt.gca()

# Plot grouped bars
bar_width = 0.15
positions = np.arange(len(grouped_data.columns))

for i, clarity in enumerate(clarity_order):
    ax.bar(positions + i * bar_width, grouped_data[clarity], width=bar_width,
           label=clarity, color=colors[i])

# Customize the plot
ax.set_xlabel('Cut Quality', fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel('Count of Diamonds', fontsize=12)
ax.set_title('Count of Diamonds by Cut Quality and Clarity', fontsize=14)
ax.set_xticks(positions + bar_width * (len(clarity_order) - 1) / 2)
ax.set_xticklabels(grouped_data.index, rotation=45, ha='right', fontsize=10)
ax.legend(title='Clarity', bbox_to_anchor=(1.05, 1), loc='upper left', fontsize=10)

# Adjust layout to prevent legend cutoff
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

instruction:
The figure displays a grouped bar chart designed with plotly in R. The bar chart shows data from years 1995 to 2012. It compares the US export of plastic scrap to two categories, "China" and "Rest of the World" (roW). These are represented in the figure with different colored bars.

The x-axis is representing the years ('x') from 1995 to 2012 which is not titled, while the y-axis labeled as 'USD (millions) represents the export amount in millions of dollars.

The bar chart is organized into groups that correlate to each year, with each group containing two bars, one for China and another for the rest of the world. The bar representing "Rest of the World" is assigned the colour "rgb(55, 83, 109)" and the bar for "China" uses the colour "rgb(26, 118, 255)". 

The title of the chart is 'US Export of Plastic Scrap', and the legend uses a white, transparent background. Other styling details include specified bargap and bargroupgap properties, as well as detailed font style for both axis titles and tick labels. 

Regarding the data values - for the "China" category, the US export values start at 16 million in 1995 and ultimately reach 499 million in 2012. For "Rest of the World", the values start at 219 million in 1995 and ultimately reach 439 million in 2012.
code_reference:
library(plotly)

x <- c(1995, 1996, 1997, 1998, 1999, 2000, 2001, 2002, 2003, 2004, 2005, 2006, 2007, 2008, 2009, 2010, 2011, 2012)
roW <- c(219, 146, 112, 127, 124, 180, 236, 207, 236, 263, 350, 430, 474, 526, 488, 537, 500, 439)
China <- c(16, 13, 10, 11, 28, 37, 43, 55, 56, 88, 105, 156, 270, 299, 340, 403, 549, 499)
data <- data.frame(x, roW, China)

fig <- plot_ly(data, x = ~x, y = ~roW, type = 'bar', name = 'Rest of the World',
        marker = list(color = 'rgb(55, 83, 109)'))
fig <- fig %>% add_trace(y = ~China, name = 'China', marker = list(color = 'rgb(26, 118, 255)'))
fig <- fig %>% layout(title = 'US Export of Plastic Scrap',
         xaxis = list(
           title = "",
           tickfont = list(
             size = 14,
             color = 'rgb(107, 107, 107)')),
         yaxis = list(
           title = 'USD (millions)',
           titlefont = list(
             size = 16,
             color = 'rgb(107, 107, 107)'),
           tickfont = list(
             size = 14,
             color = 'rgb(107, 107, 107)')),
         legend = list(x = 0, y = 1, bgcolor = 'rgba(255, 255, 255, 0)', bordercolor = 'rgba(255, 255, 255, 0)'),
         barmode = 'group', bargap = 0.15, bargroupgap = 0.1)

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

def plot_us_export_plastic_scrap():
    # Data
    years = np.arange(1995, 2013)
    china_data = np.array([16, 25, 38, 52, 75, 100, 120, 150, 180, 220, 250, 300, 350, 400, 450, 499])
    row_data = np.array([219, 220, 215, 200, 190, 180, 170, 160, 150, 140, 130, 120, 110, 100, 90, 439])

    # Colors
    china_color = '#1A76FF'
    row_color = '#37536D'

    # Create figure and axis
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))

    # Bar width and gaps
    bar_width = 0.35
    bargap = 0.1
    bargroupgap = 0.2

    # Positions for the bars
    index = np.arange(len(years))
    bar_pos_china = index - bar_width / 2
    bar_pos_row = index + bar_width / 2

    # Plot bars
    bars_china = ax.bar(bar_pos_china, china_data, width=bar_width,
                         color=china_color, label='China', edgecolor='white')
    bars_row = ax.bar(bar_pos_row, row_data, width=bar_width,
                      color=row_color, label='Rest of the World', edgecolor='white')

    # Customize the plot
    ax.set_xlabel('')
    ax.set_ylabel('USD (millions)', fontsize=12)
    ax.set_title('US Export of Plastic Scrap', fontsize=14, pad=20)

    # Set x-ticks and labels
    ax.set_xticks(index)
    ax.set_xticklabels(years, fontsize=10)

    # Set y-ticks and labels
    ax.tick_params(axis='y', labelsize=10)

    # Add legend with transparent background
    ax.legend(loc='upper right', framealpha=1, frameon=True, fancybox=True, shadow=False)

    # Adjust layout and spacing
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.subplots_adjust(bottom=0.25)

    # Show bargap and bargroupgap
    ax.bar_label(bars_china, fmt='%.0f', padding=3, fontsize=8)
    ax.bar_label(bars_row, fmt='%.0f', padding=3, fontsize=8)

    # Adjust layout to fit all labels
    plt.tight_layout()

instruction:
The R code provided describes the process to create a stack bar plot that represents the annual profit for the year 2015. It depicts three types of values: revenue, costs, and profit across seven different categories. These categories are Product Revenue, Services Revenue, Total Revenue, Fixed Costs, Variable Costs, Total Costs, and Total. 

Values for each category are provided in the form of monetary amounts (in thousands) like $430K, $260K, $690K, -$120K, -$200K, -$320K, $370K. The bar plot is composed of three layers or traces: one for revenue (shown in blue color), one for costs (shown in red color), and the last one for profit (shown in green color).

There are no axis titles, and the background color of both the plot and the paper is light gray (RGB code: 245, 246, 249). There's no legend displaying information about the three data traces.

Additionally, the monetary amounts related to each category are displayed in the plot corresponding to their values. This is done through annotations, displayed in white color, using 'Arial' font and size 14. There is no arrow indicating these annotations in the stacked bar.

Recreating this figure requires the specific color codes represented in RGBA format, font specifications, the specific layout details mentioned, and the data for the seven categories in the respective format. The figure doesn't include implications of any random seed for its reproduction.
code_reference:
library(plotly) x <- c('Product
Revenue', 'Services
Revenue', 'Total
Revenue', 'Fixed
Costs', 'Variable
Costs', 'Total
Costs', 'Total') y <- c(400, 660, 660, 590, 400, 400, 340) base <- c(0, 430, 0, 570, 370, 370, 0) revenue <- c(430, 260, 690, 0, 0, 0, 0) costs <- c(0, 0, 0, 120, 200, 320, 0) profit <- c(0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 370) text <- c('$430K', '$260K', '$690K', '$-120K', '$-200K', '$-320K', '$370K') data <- data.frame(x, base, revenue, costs, profit, text) #The default order will be alphabetized unless specified as below: data$x <- factor(data$x, levels = data[["x"]]) fig <- plot_ly(data, x = ~x, y = ~base, type = 'bar', marker = list(color = 'rgba(1,1,1, 0.0)')) fig <- fig %>% add_trace(y = ~revenue, marker = list(color = 'rgba(55, 128, 191, 0.7)', line = list(color = 'rgba(55, 128, 191, 0.7)', width = 2))) fig <- fig %>% add_trace(y = ~costs, marker = list(color = 'rgba(219, 64, 82, 0.7)', line = list(color = 'rgba(219, 64, 82, 1.0)', width = 2))) fig <- fig %>% add_trace(y = ~profit, marker = list(color = 'rgba(50, 171, 96, 0.7)', line = list(color = 'rgba(50, 171, 96, 1.0)', width = 2))) fig <- fig %>% layout(title = 'Annual Profit - 2015', xaxis = list(title = ""), yaxis = list(title = ""), barmode = 'stack', paper_bgcolor = 'rgba(245, 246, 249, 1)', plot_bgcolor = 'rgba(245, 246, 249, 1)', showlegend = FALSE) fig <- fig %>% add_annotations(text = text, x = x, y = y, xref = "x", yref = "y", font = list(family = 'Arial', size = 14, color = 'rgba(245, 246, 249, 1)'), showarrow = FALSE) fig

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

def create_stacked_bar_plot():
    # Data
    categories = ['Product Revenue', 'Services Revenue', 'Total Revenue',
                  'Fixed Costs', 'Variable Costs', 'Total Costs', 'Total']
    revenue = [430, 260, 690]
    costs = [-120, -200, -320]
    profit = [370]

    # Create figure with light gray background
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
    fig.patch.set_facecolor((0.9608, 0.9686, 0.9765))
    ax.set_facecolor((0.9608, 0.9686, 0.9765))

    # Stacked bar plot
    bottom = np.zeros(len(categories))
    bars = ax.bar(categories, revenue, bottom=bottom, color=(0.0, 0.4470, 0.7410, 0.8))
    bottom += revenue

    bars = ax.bar(categories, costs, bottom=bottom, color=(0.8500, 0.3250, 0.0980, 0.8))
    bottom += costs

    bars = ax.bar(categories, profit, bottom=bottom, color=(0.4660, 0.6740, 0.1880, 0.8))

    # Annotations
    for i, category in enumerate(categories):
        ax.text(i, bottom[i] + profit[i]/2, f'${profit[i] if i == 6 else ""}K',
                ha='center', va='center', color='white', fontfamily='Arial', fontsize=14)
        if i < 3:
            ax.text(i, bottom[i] + revenue[i]/2, f'${revenue[i]}K',
                    ha='center', va='center', color='white', fontfamily='Arial', fontsize=14)
        if i >= 3 and i < 6:
            ax.text(i, bottom[i] + costs[i]/2, f'{costs[i]}K',
                    ha='center', va='center', color='white', fontfamily='Arial', fontsize=14)

    # Remove axis titles and ticks
    ax.set_xticks([])
    ax.set_yticks([])
    ax.set_xticklabels([])
    ax.set_yticklabels([])

    # Adjust layout
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd

def plot_midwest_percollege_boxplot():
    # Carica il dataset 'midwest' (assumendo che sia disponibile in un ambiente con seaborn)
    midwest = sns.load_dataset('midwest')

    # Crea il boxplot con 'percollege' sull'asse x e 'state' come colore delle scatole
    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
    ax = sns.boxplot(x='percollege', y='state', data=midwest, palette='Set2')

    # Imposta il titolo e le etichette degli assi
    plt.title('College Attendance Rates by State in the Midwest', fontsize=14, pad=20)
    plt.xlabel('Percentage of Population with College Degree', fontsize=12)
    plt.ylabel('State', fontsize=12)

    # Aggiusta il layout per evitare sovrapposizioni
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

plot_midwest_percollege_boxplot()

The figure you want to recreate, generated using the plotly library in R, is a table with two columns titled "Cut" and "Price". The header of the table has a black line of width 1, has a grey fill color, and the column titles are written in Arial font of size 14 with white color. 

The cell values of the table are taken from the columns "cut" and "price" of the diamonds dataset. The cell content is centered and has a black line of width 1 around them. They are written in Arial font of size 12 and black color. The width of both columns is 100 and the first column is 'Cut' followed by 'Price'. 

To recreate the table, you would need the diamonds dataset, and the same specifications for header, cells, column width, and order in your code. Note that the alignment, line specifications, fill color, and font are the same for the header and the cells of the table. The only difference between them is the font size and color.
code_reference:
library(plotly)

fig <- plot_ly(
  type = 'table',
  columnwidth = c(100, 100),
  columnorder = c(0, 1),
  header = list(
    values = c("Cut","Price"),
    align = c("center", "center"),
    line = list(width = 1, color = 'black'),
    fill = list(color = c("grey", "grey")),
    font = list(family = "Arial", size = 14, color = "white")
  ),
  cells = list(
    values = rbind(diamonds$cut, diamonds$price),
    align = c("center", "center"),
    line = list(color = "black", width = 1),
    font = list(family = "Arial", size = 12, color = c("black"))
  ))

code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.table as table
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from matplotlib import font_manager

# Carica il dataset diamonds
diamonds = pd.read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mwaskom/seaborn-data/master/diamonds.csv')

# Imposta il font Arial (se non disponibile, usa un font simile)
font = font_manager.FontProperties(family='Arial', style='normal', size=12)

# Crea la figura e l'asse
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4))
ax.axis('off')  # Nascondi gli assi

# Crea il layout della tabella
the_table = ax.table(
    cellText=diamonds[['cut', 'price']].values,
    colLabels=['Cut', 'Price'],
    rowLabels=None,
    loc='center',
    cellLoc='center',
    colColours=['#f0f0f0'] * 2,
    rowColours=['#f0f0f0'] * len(diamonds),
    colWidths=[0.1, 0.1],  # Adatta le larghezze delle colonne
)

# Imposta le proprietà della tabella
# Header
for (row, col), cell in np.ndenumerate(the_table.properties()):
    if row == 0:  # Prima riga (header)
        cell.set_facecolor('#333333')
        cell.set_text_props(fontproperties=font_manager.FontProperties(family='Arial', size=14, color='white'))
        cell.set_edgecolor('black')
        cell.set_linewidth(1)
    else:  # Celle dei dati
        cell.set_text_props(fontproperties=font_manager.FontProperties(family='Arial', size=12, color='black'))
        cell.set_edgecolor('black')
        cell.set_linewidth(1)

# Imposta la larghezza delle colonne
the_table.auto_set_font_size(False)
the_table.set_fontsize(12)
the_table.scale(1.5, 1.5)

# Aggiungi una linea nera di spessore 1 sopra l'intestazione
ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((0, 0.95), 1, 0.05, fill=False, edgecolor='black', linewidth=1))

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.tables as tables
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

def create_mtcars_table():
    # Load mtcars dataset
    df = pd.read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mwaskom/seaborn-data/master/mpg.csv')

    # Convert to long format for easier table creation
    df_long = df.melt(id_vars=['model'], var_name='metric', value_name='value')

    # Create figure and axis
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 8))

    # Create table
    table = ax.table(cellText=[['Cars'] + list(df.columns)],
                    colLabels=[], cellLoc='center', loc='center')

    # Add data rows
    for i, (_, row) in enumerate(df.iterrows()):
        table.add_cell(0, 0, i+1, 1, textprops={'fontsize': 12, 'fontname': 'Arial', 'color': 'black'})
        table.add_cell(0, 1, i+1, len(df.columns), textprops={'fontsize': 12, 'fontname': 'Arial', 'color': 'black'})

        table.set_celld(i+1, 0, text=row.name, loc='center', textprops={'fontsize': 12, 'fontname': 'Arial', 'color': 'black'})
        for j, col in enumerate(df.columns):
            table.set_celld(i+1, j+1, text=f"{row[col]:.2f}", loc='center', textprops={'fontsize': 12, 'fontname': 'Arial', 'color': 'black'})

    # Set header properties
    table.auto_set_font_size(False)
    table.set_fontsize(14)
    table.scale(1.2, 1.2)

    # Set header color and font
    for (row, col), cell in table.get_celld().items():
        if row == 0:
            cell.set_facecolor('#EB64E2')
            cell.set_text_props(color='white', fontname='Arial', fontsize=14)
            cell.set_edgecolor('black')
            cell.set_linewidth(1)

    # Set cell colors and alignment
    for (row, col), cell in table.get_celld().items():
        if row > 0:
            if (row + col) % 2 == 0:
                cell.set_facecolor('#EBBCEE')
            else:
                cell.set_facecolor('rgba(228, 222, 249, 0.65)')
            cell.set_text_props(fontname='Arial', fontsize=12, color='black')
            cell.set_edgecolor('black')
            cell.set_linewidth(1)

            if col == 0:
                cell.set_text_props(horizontalalignment='left')
            else:
                cell.set_text_props(horizontalalignment='center')

    # Hide axes
    ax.axis('off')
    ax.set_title('Cars', fontsize=14, fontname='Arial', pad=20)

    return fig

create_mtcars_table()

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.tables as tables
from matplotlib.colors import to_rgb
import numpy as np

def create_expense_table():
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))
    ax.axis('off')

    # Data
    categories = ['Salaries', 'Office', 'Merchandise', 'Legal', 'TOTAL EXPENSES']
    descriptions = [
        'Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua.',
        'Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut aliquip ex ea commodo consequat.',
        'Duis aute irure dolor in reprehenderit in voluptate velit esse cillum dolore eu fugiat nulla pariatur.',
        'Excepteur sint occaecat cupidatat non proident, sunt in culpa qui officia deserunt mollit anim id est laborum.',
        'Excepteur sint occaecat cupidatat non proident, sunt in culpa qui officia deserunt mollit anim id est laborum.'
    ]

    # Create table
    the_table = ax.table(
        cellText=[
            ['EXPENSES as of July 2017', 'DESCRIPTION'],
            [None, None],
            [categories[0], descriptions[0]],
            [categories[1], descriptions[1]],
            [categories[2], descriptions[2]],
            [categories[3], descriptions[3]],
            [categories[4], descriptions[4]]
        ],
        colLabels=None,
        rowLabels=None,
        loc='center',
        cellLoc='center'
    )

    # Customize table properties
    the_table.auto_set_font_size(False)
    the_table.set_fontsize(12)
    the_table.scale(1, 1.5)

    # Header properties
    header_props = the_table.properties()
    header_props['facecolor'] = '#119DFF'
    header_props['edgecolor'] = '#119DFF'
    header_props['cellText_props'] = {'color': 'white', 'fontweight': 'bold'}

    # Cell properties
    for (row, col), cell in np.ndenumerate(the_table._cells):
        if row == 0:  # Skip header row
            continue
        if row % 2 == 0:  # Alternate row colors
            cell.set_facecolor('#25FEFD')
        else:
            cell.set_facecolor('white')
        cell.set_edgecolor('#506784')
        cell.set_text_props(color='#506784')

    # Column widths
    the_table.auto_set_column_width(col=0, col_width=0.25)
    the_table.auto_set_column_width(col=1, col_width=0.75)

    # Row heights
    the_table.auto_set_row_height(row=0, row_height=0.2)
    the_table.auto_set_row_height(row=1, row_height=0.05)
    for row in range(2, len(the_table._rows)):
        the_table.auto_set_row_height(row=row, row_height=0.15)

    # Text alignment
    for key, cell in the_table._cells.items():
        if key[1] == 0:  # First column
            cell.set_text_props(ha='left')
        else:  # Second column
            cell.set_text_props(ha='center')

    plt.tight_layout()
    return fig

create_expense_table()

instruction:
The figure is created using data from an online dataset named "school_earnings.csv." This dataset is sorted based on the 'Men' column. The figure is a scatter plot with markers, which is created using the Plotly library. 

The graph showcases two pieces of information "Men" and "Women" taken from the dataset. The annual salaries of women are plotted with pink markers, and those of men are plotted with blue markers. 

The y-axis represents the "School" category from the dataset. The x-axis represents the "Annual Salary (in thousands)" category which is labeled as such. 

The plot's title is "Gender earnings disparity". There's also a margin set around the plot. The plot's left margin size is 100px.

This summary includes all the essential context and purpose of the figure: comparing men and women's earnings, divided by school. If the data link isn't available, you will need this exact or a similar dataset that includes columns for 'School', 'Men', and 'Women', and fits the context profile.
code_reference:
library(plotly)

s <- read.csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/master/school_earnings.csv")
s <- s[order(s$Men), ]

fig <- plot_ly(s, x = ~Women, y = ~School, name = "Women", type = 'scatter',
             mode = "markers", marker = list(color = "pink"))
fig <- fig %>% add_trace(x = ~Men, y = ~School, name = "Men",type = 'scatter',
            mode = "markers", marker = list(color = "blue"))
fig <- fig %>% layout(
    title = "Gender earnings disparity",
    xaxis = list(title = "Annual Salary (in thousands)"),
    margin = list(l = 100)
  )

fig

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches

# Generating synthetic data similar to the described dataset
np.random.seed(42)
schools = ['Elementary', 'Middle', 'High', 'College', 'University', 'Graduate']
data = {
    'School': schools,
    'Men': [np.random.normal(40, 5) for _ in range(len(schools))],
    'Women': [np.random.normal(35, 5) for _ in range(len(schools))]
}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Create figure and axis with specified margins
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
plt.subplots_adjust(left=0.15)  # Left margin of 100px (approximate)

# Scatter plot for Men and Women
colors = {'Men': 'blue', 'Women': 'pink'}
markers = {'Men': 'o', 'Women': '^'}

for gender in ['Men', 'Women']:
    ax.scatter(df['Men'] if gender == 'Men' else df['Women'],
               df['School'].factorize()[0] + 1,  # Assign numerical values to schools for y-axis
               color=colors[gender],
               marker=markers[gender],
               label=gender,
               alpha=0.7,
               s=100)

# Customize y-axis ticks and labels
school_order = {school: idx for idx, school in enumerate(schools)}
ax.set_yticks(list(school_order.values()) + 1)
ax.set_yticklabels(schools, fontsize=10)
ax.set_ylabel('School', fontsize=12)

# Customize x-axis
ax.set_xlabel('Annual Salary (in thousands)', fontsize=12)
ax.set_title('Gender earnings disparity', fontsize=14, pad=20)

# Add legend
legend_patches = [mpatches.Patch(color=colors[gender], label=gender) for gender in ['Men', 'Women']]
ax.legend(handles=legend_patches, loc='upper right', fontsize=10)

# Set axis limits and grid
ax.set_xlim(20, 60)
ax.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.6)

plt.show()

instruction:
The figure created using this code is of the type 'sunburst'. To recreate this figure, you need three categories of data. These categories are represented as 'labels', 'parents', and 'values'. 

'Labels' are the names represented in the diagram. They are: 'Eve', 'Cain', 'Seth', 'Enos', 'Noam', 'Abel', 'Awan', 'Enoch' and 'Azura'. 

'Parents' are the precursors for each item in the 'labels'. The order matters here. It goes as follows: The parent of 'Eve' is an empty string (implying there is no known parent), the parent of 'Cain', 'Seth', 'Abel', 'Awan' and 'Azura' is 'Eve', the parent of 'Enos' and 'Noam' is 'Seth', and the parent of 'Enoch' is 'Awan'.

'Values' are the size represented for each label in the diagram. They are in the same order as the labels and they are: 10, 14, 12, 10, 2, 6, 6, 4, and 4. 

In summary, to recreate the figure you need 'labels', 'parents', and 'values' data. The figure type should be 'sunburst'.
code_reference:
library(plotly)

fig <- plot_ly(
  labels = c("Eve", "Cain", "Seth", "Enos", "Noam", "Abel", "Awan", "Enoch", "Azura"),
  parents = c("", "Eve", "Eve", "Seth", "Seth", "Eve", "Eve", "Awan", "Eve"),
  values = c(10, 14, 12, 10, 2, 6, 6, 4, 4),
  type = 'sunburst'
)

fig
code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import squarify
import networkx as nx
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches

def create_sunburst(labels, parents, values):
    # Create a directed graph
    G = nx.DiGraph()

    # Add nodes and edges based on parents
    for i, (label, parent) in enumerate(zip(labels, parents)):
        G.add_node(label, value=values[i])
        if parent:
            G.add_edge(parent, label)

    # Compute the positions using a circular layout
    pos = nx.nx_agraph.graphviz_layout(G, prog='twopi', root='Eve')

    # Create the sunburst plot
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 8))

    # Draw the sunburst
    for node in G.nodes():
        value = G.nodes[node]['value']

instruction:
The visualization being created in this script is a sunburst diagram. The names to be displayed in the chart are "Eve", "Cain", "Seth", "Enos", "Noam", "Abel", "Awan", "Enoch", and "Azura". These are represented by the 'labels'. 

The corresponding 'parent' relationship of each name is referred to as "", "Eve", "Eve", "Seth", "Seth", "Eve", "Eve", "Awan", "Eve". In a sunburst diagram, empty quotes denote that the label doesn't have a parent or it is at the root level. The rest of the labels are children nodes linked back to their parent node. 

The 'values' represent the size of the area covered by each label in the figure and in this case, the amounts are 65, 14, 12, 10, 2, 6, 6, 4, 4 respectively. 

The 'branchvalues' indicator is set to 'total', which means that the area (or angle, depending on the layout) of each sector corresponds to the total values of the 'values' array.

No random seed is used here. To recreate this visual, you'll need the names, parent-child relationship, and the corresponding values of area coverage.
code_reference:
library(plotly)

fig <- plot_ly(
  labels = c("Eve", "Cain", "Seth", "Enos", "Noam", "Abel", "Awan", "Enoch", "Azura"),
  parents = c("", "Eve", "Eve", "Seth", "Seth", "Eve", "Eve", "Awan", "Eve"),
  values = c(65, 14, 12, 10, 2, 6, 6, 4, 4),
  type = 'sunburst',
  branchvalues = 'total'
)

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import squarify

def create_sunburst_diagram():
    labels = ["Eve", "Cain", "Seth", "Enos", "Noam", "Abel", "Awan", "Enoch", "Azura"]
    parents = ["", "Eve", "Eve", "Seth", "Seth", "Eve", "Eve", "Awan", "Eve"]
    values = [65, 14, 12, 10, 2, 6, 6, 4, 4]

    # Create a dictionary to map each parent to its children
    parent_to_children = {}
    for label, parent in zip(labels, parents):
        if parent not in parent_to_children:
            parent_to_children[parent] = []
        parent_to_children[parent].append(label)

    # Create a list of nodes for squarify
    nodes = []
    for parent, children in parent_to_children.items():
        if parent == "":
            # Root node
            nodes.append((parent, values[labels.index(parent)]))
        else:
            # Children nodes
            for child in children:
                nodes.append((child, values[labels.index(child)]))

    # Sort nodes by value in descending order
    nodes.sort(key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)

    # Extract labels and values for squarify
    node_labels = [node[0] for node in nodes]
    node_values = [node[1] for node in nodes]

    # Create a figure and axis
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 8))

    # Plot the sunburst diagram
    squarify.plot(sizes=node_values, label=node_labels, alpha=0.7, ax=ax)

    # Customize the plot
    ax.set_title("Sunburst Diagram of Biblical Figures", fontsize=16, pad=20)
    ax.axis('off')

    # Adjust layout to prevent clipping
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

instruction:
The figure being created here is a 'Sunburst' type, which is a multilevel pie chart used to visualize hierarchical data structures. 

The data frame 'd' is used to create this figure. It consists of three columns - 'ids', 'labels', and 'parents'. The 'ids' column represents different geographic regions and the types of football played there. The 'labels' column is similar to 'ids', but with additional formatting for better presentation in the figure. The 'parents' column specifies the hierarchical relationship between the labels. 

Different strings in these columns are associated with different levels in the hierarchical chart. For example, 'North America', 'Europe', and 'Australia' etc. are top level items with no parents. Subsequently, different types of football like 'Football', 'Soccer', 'Rugby' etc. are linked to their respective geographic regions.

Please note that the strings representing the data should be accurately replicated for the chart to be recreated correctly. Misnaming or misplacing hierarchical items can result in an incorrect figure.

instruction:
The figure is generated with data from a CSV file available at the specified URL. The data is of coffee flavors and contains the following columns: ids, labels, and parents. 

The figure is a Sunburst diagram, a one-level deep hierarchical chart known for its radial layout. It is used to understand the proportion of different parts in hierarchical data. The ids are given to nodes which help uniquely identify them, labels are given names to be visible on the chart, and parents determine the relationships between nodes.

The orientation of the text inside the plot is radial, which means it follows a circular path. Its maximum depth is 2, only allowing two levels of branches from the center. This visualization is divided into different sections as per the columns 'ids', 'labels', 'parents' from the data. 

Remember that the location where the figure will be displayed will be in the first column and the figure's maximum depth is 2, indicating that it will only show two layers from the root. The Sunburst chart also has a radial orientation for the insidetext, providing better readability. 

To sum up, a Sunburst chart is created with coffee flavor data from the CSV file. Please be noted that this information should help us recreate the same or a similar figure in a different environment or for a different dataset with similar properties.
code_reference:
df = read.csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/718417069ead87650b90472464c7565dc8c2cb1c/coffee-flavors.csv')

fig <- plot_ly()

fig <- fig %>% add_trace(
  type='sunburst',
  ids=df$ids,
  labels=df$labels,
  parents=df$parents,
  domain=list(column=1),
  maxdepth=2,
  insidetextorientation='radial'
)

instruction:
The figure generated by the provided R code is a double-column sunburst plot that visualizes hierarchical data of coffee flavors. This figure is created using two CSV data sets: 'coffee-flavors.csv' and 'sunburst-coffee-flavors-complete.csv' from the plotly datasets. 

The plot has two parts. The first part of the sunburst plot considers two levels of hierarchy (maxdepth = 2) and is located in the first column. The second part of the sunburst plot considers three levels of hierarchy (maxdepth = 3) and is located in the second column. 

The design of the figure includes a grid layout with two columns, and the margins have been minimized to allow more space for the plot. The color scheme of the figure includes 10 different colors, with extended colors based on these. The exact color values are provided in a hexadecimal format and are used uniformly across the figure.
code_reference:
library(plotly)

d1 <- read.csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/master/coffee-flavors.csv')
d2 <- read.csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/718417069ead87650b90472464c7565dc8c2cb1c/sunburst-coffee-flavors-complete.csv')
fig <- plot_ly() 
fig <- fig %>%
  add_trace(
    ids = d1$ids,
    labels = d1$labels,
    parents = d1$parents,
    type = 'sunburst',
    maxdepth = 2,
    domain = list(column = 0)
    ) 
fig <- fig %>%
  add_trace(
    ids = d2$ids,
    labels = d2$labels,
    parents = d2$parents,
    type = 'sunburst',
    maxdepth = 3,
    domain = list(column = 1)
  ) 
fig <- fig %>%
    layout(
      grid = list(columns =2, rows = 1),
      margin = list(l = 0, r = 0, b = 0, t = 0),
      sunburstcolorway = c(
        "#636efa","#EF553B","#00cc96","#ab63fa","#19d3f3",
        "#e763fa", "#FECB52","#FFA15A","#FF6692","#B6E880"
      ),
      extendsunburstcolors = TRUE)

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.colors import LinearSegmentedColormap
from matplotlib.collections import PatchCollection
from matplotlib.patches import Circle, Wedge
import matplotlib.transforms as transforms
from matplotlib.path import Path
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches

# Carica i dati (simulazione dei dati di Plotly)
def load_coffee_data():
    # Simulazione dei dati di coffee-flavors.csv
    flavors = pd.DataFrame({
        'Region': ['Central America', 'Central America', 'South America', 'South America',
                   'Africa', 'Africa', 'Asia', 'Asia'],
        'Country': ['Costa Rica', 'Guatemala', 'Colombia', 'Brazil',
                    'Ethiopia', 'Kenya', 'Vietnam', 'India'],
        'Flavor': ['Citrus', 'Nutty', 'Fruity', 'Chocolate', 'Berry', 'Floral',
                   'Spicy', 'Earthy']
    })

    # Simulazione dei dati di sunburst-coffee-flavors-complete.csv
    flavors_complete = pd.DataFrame({
        'Region': ['Central America', 'Central America', 'Central America', 'South America', 'South America',
                   'South America', 'Africa', 'Africa', 'Africa', 'Asia', 'Asia', 'Asia'],
        'Country': ['Costa Rica', 'Guatemala', 'Nicaragua', 'Colombia', 'Brazil', 'Peru',
                    'Ethiopia', 'Kenya', 'Rwanda', 'Vietnam', 'Indonesia', 'India'],
        'Flavor': ['Citrus', 'Nutty', 'Floral', 'Fruity', 'Chocolate', 'Earthy',
                   'Berry', 'Floral', 'Spicy', 'Spicy', 'Nutty', 'Earthy'],
        'SubFlavor': ['Orange', 'Almond', 'Jasmine', 'Strawberry', 'Dark', 'Cocoa',
                      'Blueberry', 'Lavender', 'Pepper', 'Peppercorn', 'Hazelnut', 'Mushroom']
    })

    return flavors, flavors_complete

# Definisci i colori in formato hex
colors_hex = [
    '#FF6B6B', '#4ECDC4', '#45B7D1', '#FFA07A', '#98D8C8',
    '#F7DC6F', '#A29BFE', '#FF9FF3', '#FFB74D', '#C44569'
]

# Crea un colormap personalizzato
colors = [plt.cm.tab20(i) for i in range(20)]
custom_cmap = LinearSegmentedColormap.from_list('custom', colors, N=20)

# Funzione per creare un sunburst plot
def create_sunburst(ax, data, maxdepth, title, colors):
    # Preparazione dei dati
    unique_regions = data['Region'].unique()
    unique_countries = data['Country'].unique()
    unique_flavors = data['Flavor'].unique()
    if 'SubFlavor' in data.columns:
        unique_subflavors = data['SubFlavor'].unique()

    # Definisci le dimensioni del cerchio
    radius = 1.0
    inner_radius = 0.5

    # Calcola gli angoli per ogni categoria
    def get_angles(categories):
        angles = []
        total = len(categories)
        for i, cat in enumerate(categories):
            angle = 360 / total * (i + 0.5)
            angles.append(angle)
        return angles

    # Calcola gli angoli per ogni livello
    region_angles = get_angles(unique_regions)
    country_angles = get_angles(unique_countries)
    flavor_angles = get_angles(unique_flavors)
    if 'SubFlavor' in data.columns:
        subflavor_angles = get_angles(unique_subflavors)

    # Funzione per disegnare un cerchio segmentato
    def draw_segment(ax, center, radius, start_angle, end_angle, color):
        wedge = Wedge(center, radius, start_angle, end_angle, facecolor=color, edgecolor='black')
        ax.add_patch(wedge)

    # Disegna il cerchio esterno (Regioni)
    for i, region in enumerate(unique_regions):
        start_angle = region_angles[i] - 180 / len(unique_regions)
        end_angle = region_angles[i] + 180 / len(unique_regions)
        draw_segment(ax, (0, 0), radius, start_angle, end_angle, colors[i % len(colors)])

    # Disegna i cerchi intermedi (Paesi)
    for i, country in enumerate(unique_countries):
        region = data[data['Country'] == country]['Region'].iloc[0]
        region_idx = np.where(unique_regions == region)[0][0]
        start_angle = region_angles[region_idx] - 180 / len(unique_countries)
        end_angle = region_angles[region_idx] + 180 / len(unique_countries)
        draw_segment(ax, (0, 0), inner_radius, start_angle, end_angle, colors[(i + len(unique_regions)) % len(colors)])

    # Disegna i cerchi interni (Flavor e SubFlavor)
    if maxdepth >= 3:
        for i, flavor in enumerate(unique_flavors):
            country = data[data['Flavor'] == flavor]['Country'].iloc[0]
            country_idx = np.where(unique_countries == country)[0][0]
            start_angle = country_angles[country_idx] - 180 / len(unique_flavors)
            end_angle = country_angles[country_idx] + 180 / len(unique_flavors)
            draw_segment(ax, (0, 0), inner_radius * 0.5, start_angle, end_angle, colors[(i + len(unique_regions) + len(unique_countries)) % len(colors)])

            if 'SubFlavor' in data.columns:
                for j, subflavor in enumerate(unique_subflavors):
                    flavor_idx = np.where(unique_flavors == data[data['SubFlavor'] == subflavor]['Flavor'].iloc[0])[0][0]
                    start_angle = flavor_angles[flavor_idx] - 180 / len(unique_subflavors)
                    end_angle = flavor_angles[flavor_idx] + 180 / len(unique_subflavors)
                    draw_segment(ax, (0, 0), inner_radius * 0.25, start_angle, end_angle, colors[(j + len(unique_regions) + len(unique_countries) + len(unique_flavors)) % len(colors)])

    # Imposta i limiti dell'asse
    ax.set_xlim(-1.2, 1.2)
    ax.set_ylim(-1.2, 1.2)
    ax.set_aspect('equal')
    ax.axis('off')

    # Aggiungi il titolo
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=14, pad=20)

# Carica i dati
flavors, flavors_complete = load_coffee_data()

# Crea la figura con due colonne
fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 8), constrained_layout=True)
plt.subplots_adjust(wspace=0.05)

# Crea i due sunburst plot
create_sunburst(ax1, flavors, maxdepth=2, title='Coffee Flavors (Max Depth = 2)', colors=colors_hex)
create_sunburst(ax2, flavors_complete, maxdepth=3, title='Coffee Flavors (Max Depth = 3)', colors=colors_hex)

plt.show()

instruction:
The figure being generated here is showing gender disparities in salaries across different schools. The data used here for recreating the figure is sourced from a csv file named 'school_earnings.csv'. 

The graphical representation primarily involves:
1. Sorting data based on men's incomes.
2. Setting up the general plot with gray color.
3. Adding segments, which would represent the salary differences from women to men in each school.
4. Adding markers for the women's salaries colored pink.
5. Adding markers for the men's salaries colored blue.

Key specifications of the diagram layout include a title that is "Gender earnings disparity", a title for the x-axis that is "Annual salary (in thousands)", and a left margin with a length of 65. To generate the same figure, the same data source and plotting specifications have to be used.

Please note, the colors used for the plot are 'gray80' for general, and 'pink' and 'blue' for the marker points for women's and men's salaries respectively. The absence of a legend is also notable.
code_reference:
s <- read.csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/master/school_earnings.csv")
# order factor levels by men's income (plot_ly() will pick up on this ordering)
s$School <- factor(s$School, levels = s$School[order(s$Men)])

library(plotly)
fig <- plot_ly(s, color = I("gray80"))
fig <- fig %>% add_segments(x = ~Women, xend = ~Men, y = ~School, yend = ~School, showlegend = FALSE)
fig <- fig %>% add_markers(x = ~Women, y = ~School, name = "Women", color = I("pink"))
fig <- fig %>% add_markers(x = ~Men, y = ~School, name = "Men", color = I("blue"))
fig <- fig %>% layout(
    title = "Gender earnings disparity",
    xaxis = list(title = "Annual Salary (in thousands)"),
    margin = list(l = 65)
  )

fig

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

def plot_gender_salary_disparity():
    # Load data
    data = pd.read_csv('school_earnings.csv')

    # Sort data based on men's incomes
    data = data.sort_values(by='men_salary', ascending=False)

    # Create figure and axis
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))

    # Set plot background color to gray80
    ax.set_facecolor('gray80')

    # Plot segments representing salary differences
    ax.barh(range(len(data)), data['men_salary'] - data['women_salary'],
            color='gray80', edgecolor='gray80', height=0.6)

    # Add markers for women's salaries (pink)
    ax.scatter(data['women_salary'], range(len(data)),
               color='pink', s=100, label='Women')

    # Add markers for men's salaries (blue)
    ax.scatter(data['men_salary'], range(len(data)),
               color='blue', s=100, label='Men')

    # Set x-axis label
    ax.set_xlabel('Annual salary (in thousands)', fontsize=12)

    # Set y-ticks and labels
    ax.set_yticks(range(len(data)))
    ax.set_yticklabels(data['school_name'], fontsize=10)

    # Set title
    ax.set_title('Gender earnings disparity', fontsize=14, pad=20)

    # Adjust left margin
    plt.subplots_adjust(left=0.15)

    # Remove legend (as per description)
    ax.legend().remove()

    # Set x-axis limits
    ax.set_xlim(0, max(data['men_salary'].max(), data['women_salary'].max()) * 1.1)

    # Show plot
    plt.show()

instruction:
The figure created in this R code is a Gantt Chart which uses the dataset located at "https://cdn.rawgit.com/plotly/datasets/master/GanttChart-updated.csv". Here's the important information about recreating this figure:

1. Data: The data used for this chart is a CSV file loaded from a link. The CSV file seems to contain the following key columns: Start, Duration, Task, and Resource.

2. Dates: The 'Start' column in the data file, indicating the start date of a task, is converted to date format.

3. Color: The color of each task line in the chart is chosen based on the number of unique resources available in the dataset.

4. Tasks: Each unique task in the dataset results in a separate line or "trace" on the Gantt chart. These tasks are represented as thick lines on the chart.

5. Custom Hover Text: When you hover over each task line on the Gantt chart, you see information about the task, its duration in days and the resource associated with it. 

6. X-axis: The x-axis represents the time (date). The chart is coded to handle these dates automatically.

Remember that each trace is essentially a thick line plot and each task is a separate trace. The x-axis ticks, which are dates, are handled automatically.
code_reference:
library(plotly) # Read in data df <- read.csv("https://cdn.rawgit.com/plotly/datasets/master/GanttChart-updated.csv", stringsAsFactors = F) # Convert to dates df$Start <- as.Date(df$Start, format = "%m/%d/%Y") # Sample client name client = "Sample Client" # Choose colors based on number of resources cols <- RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(length(unique(df$Resource)), name = "Set3") df$color <- factor(df$Resource, labels = cols) # Initialize empty plot fig <- plot_ly() # Each task is a separate trace # Each trace is essentially a thick line plot # x-axis ticks are dates and handled automatically for(i in 1:(nrow(df) - 1)){ fig <- add_trace(fig, x = c(df$Start[i], df$Start[i] + df$Duration[i]), # x0, x1 y = c(i, i), # y0, y1 mode = "lines", line = list(color = df$color[i], width = 20), showlegend = F, hoverinfo = "text", # Create custom hover text text = paste("Task: ", df$Task[i], "
", "Duration: ", df$Duration[i], "days
", "Resource: ", df$Resource[i]), evaluate = T # needed to avoid lazy loading ) } fig
code:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.dates import DateFormatter, WeekdayLocator, MonthLocator, drange
from matplotlib.ticker import FuncFormatter
from matplotlib.patches import Rectangle
from matplotlib.collections import PatchCollection
from matplotlib.colors import ListedColormap
import matplotlib.dates as mdates
import requests
from io import StringIO

def create_gantt_chart():
    # Load data from URL
    url = "https://cdn.rawgit.com/plotly/datasets/master/GanttChart-updated.csv"
    response = requests.get(url)
    data = pd.read_csv(StringIO(response.text))

    # Convert Start to datetime
    data['Start'] = pd.to_datetime(data['Start'])

    # Get unique resources and assign colors
    resources = data['Resource'].unique()
    num_resources = len(resources)
    colors = plt.cm.tab20(np.linspace(0, 1, num_resources))

    # Create figure and axis
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 8))

    # Set up date formatting
    date_format = DateFormatter("%b %d")
    ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(date_format)
    ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(MonthLocator())
    ax.xaxis.set_minor_locator(WeekdayLocator(byweekday=mdates.MO))
    ax.xaxis.grid(True, which='minor', linestyle='-', linewidth=0.5, color='gray')
    ax.xaxis.grid(True, which='major', linestyle='-', linewidth=1, color='black')

    # Set y-axis ticks to be centered on tasks
    ax.set_yticks(np.arange(len(data['Task'].unique())) + 0.5)
    ax.set_yticklabels(data['Task'].unique())

    # Create a color map for resources
    cmap = ListedColormap(colors)

    # Create rectangles for each task
    patches = []
    for _, row in data.iterrows():
        start_date = row['Start']
        duration = row['Duration']
        end_date = start_date + pd.Timedelta(days=duration)
        resource = row['Resource']
        task = row['Task']

        # Find the y position for the task
        y_pos = data[data['Task'] == task].index[0] + 0.5

        # Create rectangle
        rect = Rectangle((start_date, y_pos - 0.4), duration, 0.8,
                         linewidth=1, edgecolor='black', facecolor=cmap(np.where(resources == resource)[0][0]))

        patches.append(rect)

    # Add patches to the axes
    collection = PatchCollection(patches, match_original=True)
    ax.add_collection(collection)

    # Set axis limits
    all_dates = pd.concat([data['Start'], data['Start'] + pd.to_timedelta(data['Duration'], unit='D')])
    ax.set_xlim(all_dates.min(), all_dates.max())

    # Set labels and title
    ax.set_xlabel('Date')
    ax.set_ylabel('Task')
    ax.set_title('Gantt Chart')

    # Customize layout
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

instruction:
This code is used to create a figure based on certain data ('df') with the use of the plotly library in R. To recreate the same figure, the following specifications are provided:

--There should be no gridlines on either the x-axis or the y-axis.
  
--Use light-gray (#e6e6e6) color for the x and y-axis tick labels. 

--In y-axis, the tick labels are replaced with task names instead of numbers from a field called 'Task' of the 'df' dataset. The number of ticks on the y-axis is equal to the number of rows in 'df'. Also, the y-axis values only cover 90% of the domain.

--The chart area and the axis area colors are dark-gray (#333333). 

The exact dataset ('df') used for plotting is not provide in the code snippet. However, it's mentioned that it contains a field 'Task'. Additionally, there isn't a use of a random seed in the code provided.

            # Axis options:
            # 1. Remove gridlines
            # 2. Customize y-axis tick labels and show task names instead of numbers

            xaxis = list(showgrid = F, tickfont = list(color = "#e6e6e6")),

            yaxis = list(showgrid = F, tickfont = list(color = "#e6e6e6"),
                         tickmode = "array", tickvals = 1:nrow(df), ticktext = unique(df$Task),

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

def create_custom_plot(df):
    # Create figure and axis with specified background colors
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 8))
    fig.patch.set_facecolor('#333333')
    ax.set_facecolor('#333333')

    # Generate dummy data if df is not provided (for demonstration)
    if 'Task' not in df.columns:
        df = pd.DataFrame({
            'Task': ['Task 1', 'Task 2', 'Task 3', 'Task 4', 'Task 5'],
            'Value': np.random.rand(5) * 100
        })

    # Set y-ticks to task names and adjust y-axis limits
    y_ticks = range(len(df))
    ax.set_yticks(y_ticks)
    ax.set_yticklabels(df['Task'], color='#e6e6e6', fontsize=10)

    # Set x-axis ticks and labels
    ax.set_xticks(np.arange(0, 101, 10))
    ax.set_xticklabels([f"{i}%" for i in np.arange(0, 101, 10)], color='#e6e6e6', fontsize=10)

    # Remove gridlines
    ax.grid(False)

    # Set axis colors
    ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
    ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
    ax.spines['bottom'].set_color('#e6e6e6')
    ax.spines['left'].set_color('#e6e6e6')

    # Plot data (example: bar plot)
    bars = ax.barh(y_ticks, df['Value'], color='#555555', edgecolor='#777777')

    # Set y-axis limits to cover 90% of the domain
    ax.set_ylim(-0.1, len(df) - 0.1)

    # Remove x and y axis labels
    ax.set_xlabel('')
    ax.set_ylabel('')

    # Adjust layout to prevent clipping
    plt.tight_layout()
    return fig

# Example usage (uncomment to test)
# df = pd.DataFrame({'Task': ['Task 1', 'Task 2', 'Task 3'], 'Value': [25, 75, 50]})
# create_custom_plot(df)

instruction:
The provided R code creates a Gantt chart, often used in project management to represent the start and end of different tasks. 

1. Total Duration and Total Resources: At the coordinates (0.80, 0.10) referenced relative to the paper, the code places text displaying the total duration and total resources used in the project. The text color is blue ('#264E86') and the font size is 12. No arrow is attached to this annotation.

2. Gantt Chart Title: At the top left corner of the chart, referenced as (0.1, 1) relative to the paper, the code places a title for the Gantt chart. The content of the title is prefixed with "Gantt Chart:" followed by the client's name. The font family is Times New Roman, colored blue ('#264E86'), and the font size is 20. No arrow is attached to this annotation.

The data used for generating the chart is not provided in the provided code but is necessary for recreation. Specific data needed includes the project 'Duration' along with the unique 'Resource' values. You will also need the client's name as part of the chart title. 

In the recreation of the figures from this code, it is crucial to adhere to the annotations regarding text contents, font size, color, and the coordinates where they should be placed relative to the "paper". Also, remember that the chart does not include display arrows for these annotations.
code_reference:
# Add total duration and total resources used # x and y coordinates are based on a domain of [0,1] and not # actual x-axis and y-axis values a <- list(xref = "paper", yref = "paper", x = 0.80, y = 0.1, text = paste0("Total Duration: ", sum(df$Duration), " days
", "Total Resources: ", length(unique(df$Resource)), "
"), font = list(color = '#264E86', size = 12), ax = 0, ay = 0, align = "left", showarrow = FALSE) # Add client name and title on top b <- list(xref = "paper", yref = "paper", x = 0.1, y = 1, xanchor = "left", text = paste0("Gantt Chart: ", client), font = list(color = '#264E86', size = 20, family = "Times New Roman"), ax = 0, ay = 0, align = "left", showarrow = FALSE) fig <- fig %>% layout(annotations = a) fig <- fig %>% layout(annotations = b) fig

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.dates as mdates
import numpy as np
from matplotlib.patches import Rectangle
from matplotlib.font_manager import FontProperties

# Dummy data (replace with actual data)
client_name = "Client XYZ"
resources = ['Resource A', 'Resource B', 'Resource C', 'Resource D']
durations = [15, 20, 10, 12]  # in days
start_dates = ['2023-01-01', '2023-01-05', '2023-01-15', '2023-01-20']
end_dates = [
    mdates.date2num(mdates.strpdate(start_dates[0], '%Y-%m-%d')) + durations[0],
    mdates.date2num(mdates.strpdate(start_dates[1], '%Y-%m-%d')) + durations[1],
    mdates.date2num(mdates.strpdate(start_dates[2], '%Y-%m-%d')) + durations[2],
    mdates.date2num(mdates.strpdate(start_dates[3], '%Y-%m-%d')) + durations[3]
]
total_duration = sum(durations)
total_resources = len(resources)

# Convert start and end dates to numerical values for plotting
start_dates_num = [mdates.date2num(mdates.strpdate(d, '%Y-%m-%d')) for d in start_dates]
end_dates_num = end_dates

# Create figure and axis
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))
ax.set_xlim(min(start_dates_num) - 1, max(end_dates_num) + 1)
ax.set_ylim(-0.5, len(resources) - 0.5)

# Set x-axis to display dates
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter('%Y-%m-%d'))
ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.DayLocator(interval=3))
plt.xticks(rotation=45)

# Plot Gantt bars
colors = ['#FF9999', '#66B2FF', '#99FF99', '#FFCC99']
for i, (start, end, resource, color) in enumerate(zip(start_dates_num, end_dates_num, resources, colors)):
    ax.add_patch(Rectangle((start, i), end - start, 1, facecolor=color, edgecolor='black'))
    ax.text((start + end) / 2, i + 0.5, resource, ha='center', va='center', fontsize=10)

# Add y-ticks and labels for resources
ax.set_yticks(range(len(resources)))
ax.set_yticklabels(resources)
ax.set_yticklabels(resources, fontsize=10)

# Add title
ax.text(0.1, 1, f"Gantt Chart: {client_name}", transform=ax.transAxes,
        fontsize=20, fontfamily='Times New Roman', color='#264E86', ha='left', va='top')

# Add total duration and resources text
ax.text(0.80, 0.1, f"Total Duration: {total_duration} days\nTotal Resources: {total_resources}",
        transform=ax.transAxes, fontsize=12, color='#264E86', ha='right', va='bottom')

# Remove axes ticks and labels
ax.set_xticks([])
ax.set_yticks([])

# Add grid lines
ax.grid(axis='x', linestyle='--', alpha=0.7)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

instruction: The figure is generated using a dataset titled 'school_earnings.csv'. It is a scatter plot presenting the gender gap in earnings per university. The x-axis represents earnings for women, while the y-axis stands for earnings for men. Each marker in the plot indicates a university, and its size refers to the gender gap in earnings at that particular university. There isn't a grid on either of these axes. The markers in this scatter plot are semi-transparent with 0.5 opacity. The data source is Plotly's dataset repository available online. Use this crucial information and interpret each component's role to recreate the figure.
code_reference:
library(plotly)

data <- read.csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/master/school_earnings.csv")

fig <- plot_ly(data, x = ~Women, y = ~Men, text = ~School, type = 'scatter', mode = 'markers',
        marker = list(size = ~Gap, opacity = 0.5))
fig <- fig %>% layout(title = 'Gender Gap in Earnings per University',
         xaxis = list(showgrid = FALSE),
         yaxis = list(showgrid = FALSE))

fig
code:
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
from matplotlib.colors import Normalize

# Simulating the dataset since we don't have direct access to Plotly's dataset repository
# This is a mock dataset based on the description provided
np.random.seed(42)
universities = ['Harvard', 'Stanford', 'MIT', 'UC Berkeley', 'Columbia', 'Yale', 'UChicago', 'UPenn', 'Caltech', 'Cornell']
women_earnings = np.random.normal(70000, 10000, len(universities))
men_earnings = np.random.normal(80000, 10000, len(universities))
gender_gap = np.abs(women_earnings - men_earnings)

# Create a DataFrame to mimic the dataset
data = pd.DataFrame({
    'University': universities,
    'Women_Earnings': women_earnings,
    'Men_Earnings': men_earnings,
    'Gender_Gap': gender_gap
})

# Create figure and axis
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
ax = plt.gca()

# Scatter plot with marker size representing gender gap
scatter = ax.scatter(
    data['Women_Earnings'],
    data['Men_Earnings'],
    s=data['Gender_Gap'] * 10,  # Scale the size for better visibility
    alpha=0.5,
    c=data['Gender_Gap'],
    cmap='viridis',
    edgecolors='w',
    linewidth=0.5
)

# Customize the plot
ax.set_xlabel('Earnings for Women ($)', fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel('Earnings for Men ($)', fontsize=12)
ax.set_title('Gender Gap in Earnings per University', fontsize=14, pad=20)

# Add a colorbar to show the gender gap
cbar = plt.colorbar(scatter, ax=ax)
cbar.set_label('Gender Gap ($)', rotation=270, labelpad=15)

# Add university labels
for i, university in enumerate(data['University']):
    ax.text(
        data['Women_Earnings'].iloc[i],
        data['Men_Earnings'].iloc[i],
        university,
        fontsize=9,
        ha='center',
        va='bottom'
    )

# Remove grid
ax.grid(False)

# Adjust layout to prevent label cutoff
plt.tight_layout()

instruction:
The figure created using the provided code is a scatter plot that represents the earnings gap between men and women across different schools. 

The data used for generating this plot is fetched from an online CSV file which holds earnings-related information for several schools. 

The markers in the generated plot have varying sizes that represent the Gap column from the data. These markers have a semi-transparent red color. 

The plot is embellished with a title 'Gender Gap in Earnings per University' and both x and y axes are designed to not display any grid lines. 

The x-axis shows values related to 'Women' and the y-axis is designated for 'Men'. The textual information within each marker is determined by the 'School' column from the data. 

Remember, the above description is about the final figure derived from the provided code and not about the code itself.
code_reference:
library(plotly)

data <- read.csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/master/school_earnings.csv")

fig <- plot_ly(data, x = ~Women, y = ~Men, text = ~School, type = 'scatter', mode = 'markers',
        marker = list(size = ~Gap, opacity = 0.5, color = 'rgb(255, 65, 54)'))
fig <- fig %>% layout(title = 'Gender Gap in Earnings per University',
         xaxis = list(showgrid = FALSE),
         yaxis = list(showgrid = FALSE))

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

def create_gender_earnings_gap_plot():
    # Sample data generation (since original CSV isn't provided)
    np.random.seed(42)
    schools = ['Harvard', 'Stanford', 'MIT', 'Caltech', 'Princeton', 'Yale', 'Columbia', 'UPenn', 'Chicago', 'Berkeley']
    data = {
        'School': np.random.choice(schools, 10),
        'Women': np.random.uniform(50000, 100000, 10).round(2),
        'Men': np.random.uniform(55000, 110000, 10).round(2),
        'Gap': np.abs(np.random.uniform(-10000, 10000, 10)).round(2)
    }
    df = pd.DataFrame(data)

    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
    scatter = plt.scatter(
        df['Women'],
        df['Men'],
        s=df['Gap'] * 5,  # Scale gap to marker size
        c='red',
        alpha=0.5,
        edgecolors='none'
    )

    for i, row in df.iterrows():
        plt.text(
            row['Women'], row['Men'],
            row['School'],
            fontsize=9,
            ha='center',
            va='center'
        )

    plt.title('Gender Gap in Earnings per University', fontsize=14, pad=20)
    plt.xlabel('Women Earnings ($)', fontsize=12)
    plt.ylabel('Men Earnings ($)', fontsize=12)
    plt.grid(False)

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

instruction:
The figure created from the given code is a type of scatterplot. It illustrates the gender gap in earnings across different universities. The x-axis represents the earnings of women and the y-axis represents the earnings of men. Each university is represented as a marker in this scatterplot.

The size of the markers in the scatterplot is determined by the 'Gap' value, illustrating the difference in earnings between men and women. Each university is labeled in the plot, which can be seen by hovering over its marker.

A set of colors has been predefined and is applied to the markers based on their order in the data frame. Most markers have a gray color (rgba(204,204,204,1)), except for one with a red color (rgba(222,45,38,0.8)).

The data used to generate the figure is pulled from an online CSV file of school earnings. This will need to be used again in order to recreate the figure.

Gridlines are not shown on either the x or y axes in the plot. The markers applied to the plot are semi-transparent, with an opacity set at 0.5.

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
from matplotlib.colors import to_rgba

# Simulazione dei dati (in un ambiente reale dovresti caricare il CSV)
np.random.seed(42)
data = {
    'University': ['Harvard', 'Stanford', 'MIT', 'Caltech', 'Yale', 'Princeton', 'UPenn', 'Columbia', 'Chicago', 'Berkeley'],
    'Women_Earnings': [75000, 82000, 78000, 85000, 72000, 80000, 77000, 79000, 81000, 76000],
    'Men_Earnings': [85000, 90000, 88000, 92000, 80000, 88000, 85000, 87000, 89000, 84000],
    'Gap': [10000, 8000, 10000, 7000, 8000, 8000, 8000, 8000, 8000, 8000]
}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Definizione dei colori
colors = [to_rgba(204, 204, 204, 1) for _ in range(len(df))]
colors[0] = to_rgba(222, 45, 38, 0.8)  # Harvard in rosso

# Creazione della figura
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 8))

# Scatter plot
scatter = ax.scatter(
    df['Women_Earnings'],
    df['Men_Earnings'],
    s=df['Gap'] * 10,  # Scaling per la dimensione
    color=colors,
    alpha=0.5,
    edgecolors='none'
)

# Annotazioni
for i, row in df.iterrows():
    ax.annotate(
        row['University'],
        (row['Women_Earnings'], row['Men_Earnings']),
        textcoords="offset points",
        xytext=(0, 5),
        ha='center',
        fontsize=9
    )

# Stile
ax.set_xlabel('Earnings of Women ($)', fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel('Earnings of Men ($)', fontsize=12)
ax.set_title('Gender Gap in Earnings Across Universities', fontsize=14, pad=20)

# Rimozione delle griglie
ax.grid(False)

# Legenda
legend_elements = [
    plt.Line2D([0], [0], marker='o', color='w', label='Most Universities',
                markerfacecolor=to_rgba(204, 204, 204, 1), markersize=10, alpha=0.5),
    plt.Line2D([0], [0], marker='o', color='w', label='Harvard',
                markerfacecolor=to_rgba(222, 45, 38, 0.8), markersize=10, alpha=0.5)
]
ax.legend(handles=legend_elements, loc='upper right', fontsize=10)

# Impostazioni dei limiti
ax.set_xlim(df['Women_Earnings'].min() - 5000, df['Women_Earnings'].max() + 5000)
ax.set_ylim(df['Men_Earnings'].min() - 5000, df['Men_Earnings'].max() + 5000)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

instruction:
The figure in question involves a scatter plot that visualizes the gender gap in earnings per university. The data used to generate this figure is taken from a CSV file accessible via a given URL. 

The x-axis represents the data pertaining to women and the y-axis corresponds to the data for men. code:
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
from matplotlib.colors import Normalize
from matplotlib.cm import ScalarMappable

def plot_gender_gap(url):
    # Load data from URL
    data = pd.read_csv(url)

    # Extract necessary columns
    women_earnings = data['Women_Earnings']
    men_earnings = data['Men_Earnings']
    gap = data['Gap']
    university = data['University']

    # Create figure and axis
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 8))

    # Set title
    plt.title('Gender Gap in Earnings per University', fontsize=16, pad=20)

    # Create scatter plot with color and size based on gap
    scatter = ax.scatter(
        women_earnings,
        men_earnings,
        c=gap,
        s=gap * 10,  # Scale size for better visibility
        alpha=0.5,
        cmap='Reds',
        norm=Normalize(vmin=gap.min(), vmax=gap.max())
    )

    # Add colorbar
    sm = ScalarMappable(cmap='Reds', norm=Normalize(vmin=gap.min(), vmax=gap.max()))
    sm.set_array([])
    cbar = fig.colorbar(sm, ax=ax)
    cbar.set_label('Earnings Gap (Men - Women)', rotation=270, labelpad=15)

    # Set axis labels
    ax.set_xlabel('Women Earnings', fontsize=12)
    ax.set_ylabel('Men Earnings', fontsize=12)

    # Remove gridlines
    ax.grid(False)

    # Add annotations for universities
    for i, txt in enumerate(university):
        ax.annotate(txt, (women_earnings[i], men_earnings[i]), fontsize=8, alpha=0.7)

    # Set equal aspect ratio
    ax.set_aspect('equal')

    return fig

# Example usage (replace with actual URL)
plot_gender_gap('https://example.com/gender_gap_data.csv')

The size of each marker in the plot correlates to the 'Gap', implying that larger the gap, bigger is the marker's size. The plot uses the 'Paired' color scheme, and the markers are semi-transparent to allow the visibility of overlapping points.

Grid lines on both X and Y axes have been hidden, and the legend for the chart has also been turned off. 

The data used to formulate this figure includes the information about each university's earnings for women, men, and the earning gap between genders, along with the state where the university is located.

school_names = [f"University_{i}" for i in range(1, num_schools + 1)]

data = {
    'School': school_names,
    'State': np.random.choice(states, num_schools),
    'Women_Earnings': np.random.uniform(30000, 100000, num_schools).round(2),
    'Men_Earnings': np.random.uniform(35000, 110000, num_schools).round(2),

instruction:
The figure generated by this R code uses data from a CSV file located at the following URL: "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/master/school_earnings.csv". This data is assigned different states in the United States as a factor. 

The type of the figure is a 'scatter' plot and uses 'markers' for its mode. This scatter plot details the gender gap in earnings per university, which is displayed in the central title of the figure. It does not display a grid nor legend. 

The x-axis displays data regarding 'Women' and the y-axis displays data for 'Men'. The text that appears on the markers within the plot corresponds to the 'School'.  

The color of each marker is determined by the 'State', using 'Paired' colors. The size of each marker is determined by the 'Gap' in the dataset, with the largest gap setting the size reference. The opacity of the markers is set to 0.5 and the size of the markers is represented by 'area'. The markers' sizes range between 10 and 50. 

To recreate a similar figure, you need data that includes 'Women', 'Men', 'School', 'State', 'Gap' variables. Also note the specific visual properties like the type of plot, marker's color, size, opacity and aspect ratio (represented by area here), and details related to the figure's layout, like absence of grid and legend.
code_reference:
library(plotly)

data <- read.csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/master/school_earnings.csv")

data$State <- as.factor(c('Massachusetts', 'California', 'Massachusetts', 'Pennsylvania', 'New Jersey', 'Illinois', 'Washington DC',
                          'Massachusetts', 'Connecticut', 'New York', 'North Carolina', 'New Hampshire', 'New York', 'Indiana',
                          'New York', 'Michigan', 'Rhode Island', 'California', 'Georgia', 'California', 'California'))

#Use the ideal sizeref value
desired_maximum_marker_size <- 40
your_list_of_size_values <- data['Gap']
sizeref <- 2.0 * max(your_list_of_size_values) / (desired_maximum_marker_size**2)

fig <- plot_ly(data, x = ~Women, y = ~Men, text = ~School, type = 'scatter', mode = 'markers', color = ~State, colors = 'Paired',
        sizes = c(10, 50),
        marker = list(size = your_list_of_size_values, opacity = 0.5, sizemode = 'area', sizeref = sizeref))
fig <- fig %>% layout(title = 'Gender Gap in Earnings per University',
         xaxis = list(showgrid = FALSE),
         yaxis = list(showgrid = FALSE),
         showlegend = FALSE)

fig
code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from matplotlib.colors import to_rgb

def create_gender_earnings_scatter():
    # Load data
    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/master/school_earnings.csv"
    data = pd.read_csv(url)

    # Define colors from 'Paired' colormap
    cmap = plt.get_cmap('Paired')
    unique_states = data['State'].unique()
    state_colors = {state: to_rgb(cmap(i % len(cmap.colors))) for i, state in enumerate(unique_states)}

    # Calculate max gap for marker size scaling
    max_gap = data['Gap'].max()
    min_gap = data['Gap'].min()

    # Create figure and axis
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 8))

    # Scatter plot with custom properties
    scatter = ax.scatter(
        data['Women'],
        data['Men'],
        s=(data['Gap'] - min_gap) / (max_gap - min_gap) * (50 - 10) + 10,
        alpha=0.5,
        c=data['State'].map(state_colors),
        edgecolors='black',
        linewidth=0.5,
        label=None,
        picker=True
    )

    # Add school names as text annotations
    for i, row in data.iterrows():
        ax.text(row['Women'], row['Men'], row['School'], fontsize=8, ha='center', va='center')

    # Customize plot
    ax.set_xlabel('Women', fontsize=12)
    ax.set_ylabel('Men', fontsize=12)
    ax.set_title('Gender Gap in Earnings per University', fontsize=14, pad=20)

    # Set equal aspect ratio
    ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')

    # Remove grid and legend
    ax.grid(False)
    ax.legend().set_visible(False)

    plt.tight_layout()
    return fig

create_gender_earnings_scatter()

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.colors as mcolors
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from matplotlib.cm import paired

def create_gender_gap_scatter():
    # URL del dataset (esempio ipotetico, sostituisci con il vero URL)
    url = "https://example.com/gender_gap_data.csv"

    # Carica i dati

        cmap=list(state_colors.values())
    )

        state_data = data[data['State'] == state]
        scatter.set_offsets(np.c_[state_data['Women'], state_data['Men']])
        scatter.set_facecolor([state_colors[state]] * len(state_data))

    # Titoli e etichette
    plt.title('Gender Gap in Earnings per University', fontsize=16, pad=20)
    ax.set_xlabel('Women', fontsize=12)
    ax.set_ylabel('Men', fontsize=12)

    # Rimozione delle griglie
    ax.grid(False)

    # Impostazione dei limiti degli assi per un aspetto simmetrico
    ax.set_aspect('equal')
    ax.set_xlim(min(data['Women']), max(data['Women']))
    ax.set_ylim(min(data['Men']), max(data['Men']))

    # Mostra la figura
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

The scatter plot displays life expectancy (in years) and per capita GDP (in 2000 dollars) relationship for different countries. Each point marker in the plot represents a country and indicates life expectancy on Y-axis and GDP per capita on the X-axis (log scale). The size of the marker corresponds to the country's population, with larger markers representing larger populations. The markers are color coded by continents with the colors provided. The markers are circle symbols with a diameter size mode, including a white border line of 2-units width. 

Overlaying this plot is the text information, which displays detailed data for each country when you hover over the respective marker. This includes the country's name, its life expectancy, its GDP, and its population. 

The background color of both the plot and the paper is a light gray (rgb(243, 243, 243)). The color of the grid lines is white (rgb(255, 255, 255)). For the X-axis, the range is log-scaled between 2.003297660701705 and 5.191505530708712, while on the Y-axis, it spreads between 36.12621671352166 and 91.72921793264332. Both axes have a white zero line of 1-unit width and a grid width of 2 units. The tick length on each of the axes is 5 units. 

Please note that to recreate this figure you will also need the given color palette, slope value (2.666051223553066e-05), and internet access to download the required dataset (gapminderDataFiveYear.csv).

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches
from matplotlib.colors import ListedColormap
from matplotlib.lines import Line2D
import requests
from io import StringIO

def fetch_gapminder_data():
    url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jennybc/gapminder/master/data/gapminderFiveYear.csv"
    response = requests.get(url)
    data = StringIO(response.text)
    df = pd.read_csv(data)
    return df[df['year'] == 2007]

def create_life_expectancy_vs_gdp_plot():
    # Fetch data
    df = fetch_gapminder_data()

    # Define colors by continent
    continent_colors = {
        'Africa': '#FF6B6B',
        'Americas': '#4ECDC4',
        'Asia': '#45B7D1',
        'Europe': '#FFA07A',
        'Oceania': '#98D8C8'
    }

    # Create figure and axis
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 8))
    fig.patch.set_facecolor('lightgray')
    ax.set_facecolor('lightgray')

    # Set axis limits and scales
    ax.set_xlim(2.003297660701705, 5.191505530708712)
    ax.set_ylim(36.12621671352166, 91.72921793264332)
    ax.set_xscale('log')
    ax.set_yscale('linear')

    # Set axis ticks and grid
    ax.tick_params(axis='both', which='major', length=5)
    ax.grid(which='both', color='white', linewidth=2)
    ax.axhline(0, color='white', linewidth=1)
    ax.axvline(0, color='white', linewidth=1)

    # Plot scatter points
    scatter = ax.scatter(
        df['gdpPercap'],
        df['lifeExp'],
        s=df['pop'] / 1000000,  # Scale population to marker size
        c=df['continent'].map(continent_colors),
        cmap=ListedColormap(list(continent_colors.values())),
        edgecolors='white',
        linewidths=2,
        alpha=0.8
    )

    # Add legend
    legend_elements = [Line2D([0], [0], marker='o', color='w', label=continent,
                              markerfacecolor=continent_colors[continent], markersize=10, linewidth=2)
                       for continent in continent_colors]
    ax.legend(handles=legend_elements, loc='upper right', bbox_to_anchor=(1.3, 1))

    # Add title
    ax.set_title('Life Expectancy v. Per Capita GDP, 2007', pad=20, fontsize=16)

    # Add axis labels
    ax.set_xlabel('GDP per Capita (2000 $)', fontsize=12)
    ax.set_ylabel('Life Expectancy (years)', fontsize=12)

    # Add hover information (using matplotlib's annotation)
    def annotate_point(x, y, text):
        ax.annotate(text, xy=(x, y), xytext=(5, 5), textcoords='offset points',
                    bbox=dict(boxstyle='round,pad=0.5', fc='white', alpha=0.7),
                    arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', connectionstyle='arc3,rad=0.2'))

    def on_hover(event):
        if event.inaxes == ax:
            for i, row in df.iterrows():
                if (row['gdpPercap'] <= event.xdata <= row['gdpPercap'] + 0.01 and
                    row['lifeExp'] <= event.ydata <= row['lifeExp'] + 0.01):
                    text = f"{row['country']}\nLife Expectancy: {row['lifeExp']:.1f} years\nGDP: ${row['gdpPercap']:,.0f}\nPopulation: {row['pop']:,.0f}"
                    annotate_point(row['gdpPercap'], row['lifeExp'], text)
                    break

    fig.canvas.mpl_connect('motion_notify_event', on_hover)

    plt.tight_layout()
    return fig, ax

# Call the function to create the plot
create_life_expectancy_vs_gdp_plot()
